title: Print vs Println — spot the difference
instruction:
`fmt.Println` prints your text and then moves to the next line.
`fmt.Print` prints your text and stays on the same line.

Sounds minor. Watch what happens.

Run this and look carefully at the output.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Print("Hello ")
	fmt.Print("there ")
	fmt.Print("friend")
	fmt.Println()
	fmt.Println("Now I'm on a new line")
}
expectedOutput: Now I'm on a new line
successMessage: Print stays put. Println moves on. You will use both — now you know why they exist.

title: Printf — your first format verb
instruction:
`fmt.Printf` lets you build output by mixing text with variables using format verbs.

A format verb is a placeholder that starts with `%`. Go swaps it with the actual value.

- `%s` → a string
- `%d` → an integer
- `%f` → a float

`fmt.Printf("My name is %s and I am %d years old\n", name, age)`

Change the name and age to yours, then run it.
hint: Replace "Alex" with your name and 25 with your age. Leave the format string exactly as it is.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name := "Alex"
	age := 25
	fmt.Printf("My name is %s and I am %d years old\n", name, age)
}

codeChecks.pattern: Printf\("My name is %s and I am %d years old\\n",\s*name,\s*age\)
codeChecks.message: Keep the Printf format string as is. Only change the name and age values.
Change the name from Alex to your own name.

successMessage: That is Printf. One line, multiple values, full control over how they appear.

title: More format verbs
instruction:
Go has a format verb for almost everything.

- `%v` → the value in its default format (works on anything)
- `%T` → the type of the variable
- `%t` → a bool
- `%b` → a number in binary
- `%x` → a number in hexadecimal

Run this. One number, printed five different ways.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	n := 42
	fmt.Printf("default:  %v\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("type:     %T\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("binary:   %b\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("hex:      %x\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("decimal:  %d\n", n)
}
successMessage: 42 in binary is 101010. 42 in hex is 2a. Same number, totally different faces. Computers are wild.

title: Width and alignment
instruction:
You can tell Go exactly how wide a value should be when printed.

`%10s` → right-align in 10 characters (pad with spaces on the left)
`%-10s` → left-align in 10 characters (pad with spaces on the right)
`%05d` → pad a number with zeros on the left until it is 5 digits wide

Run this and see how things line up.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "right", "left")
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "Go", "is fun")
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "id:", "001")
	fmt.Printf("order #%05d\n", 42)
}
successMessage: Width and alignment make your output readable. Think receipts, tables, logs — anything that needs columns.

title: Decimal precision
instruction:
By default `%f` prints 6 decimal places, which is usually too many.

`%.2f` → exactly 2 decimal places
`%8.2f` → 8 characters wide, 2 decimal places

Your coffee costs $4.5, not $4.500000.

Change the format strings to show price with 2 decimal places and percentage with 1 decimal place.
hint: Use %.2f for price and %.1f for percentage. Run it and check the output.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	price := 4.5
	percentage := 87.333333
	fmt.Printf("Price: $%f\n", price)
	fmt.Printf("Score: %f%%\n", percentage)
}

codeChecks.message: Use %.2f to format the price to 2 decimal places.
Use %.1f to format the percentage to 1 decimal place.

successMessage: Clean numbers. Nobody wants to see $4.500000 on a price tag.

title: Sprintf — build a string, don't print it
instruction:
`fmt.Sprintf` works exactly like `Printf` but instead of printing, it returns the result as a string.

Why? Because sometimes you need to build a message and use it later — store it in a variable, pass it somewhere, log it.

`label := fmt.Sprintf("User #%d: %s", id, name)`

Create a `greeting` variable using `Sprintf` that says `"Welcome back, [your name]! You have 3 messages."` — then print it.
hint: greeting := fmt.Sprintf("Welcome back, %s! You have %d messages.", name, 3)  — then fmt.Println(greeting).

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Sprintf to build the greeting string.
Store the result in a variable called greeting.
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Sprintf is Printf that hands you the result instead of printing it. You will use this constantly.

title: Escape sequences
instruction:
Some characters cannot just be typed into a string. They need a special code called an escape sequence.

- `\n` → newline (go to the next line)
- `\t` → tab (big horizontal gap)
- `\\` → a literal backslash
- `\"` → a quote character inside a string

Run this and see each one in action.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Line one\nLine two")
	fmt.Println("Name:\tAlex")
	fmt.Println("Path: C:\\Users\\Alex")
	fmt.Println("She said \"hello\"")
}
successMessage: Escape sequences let you put special characters inside strings. \n and \t are the ones you will use most.

title: Println with multiple arguments
instruction:
You already know `fmt.Println(oneValue)` — but it can take multiple values at once.

`fmt.Println(a, b, c)` prints all of them on one line, separated by spaces. No format string needed.

Add `city` and `country` variables, then print all four values — `name`, `age`, `city`, `country` — in a single `Println` call.
hint: fmt.Println(name, age, city, country)  — all four in one call, separated by commas.

codeChecks.message: Create a city variable with your city.
Create a country variable with your country.
Print all four in one Println call: fmt.Println(name, age, city, country)
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Println handles multiple values and inserts spaces automatically. Quick and clean.

title: Printf with multiple verbs
instruction:
A single `Printf` can hold as many format verbs as you need.

The values you pass after the format string fill in the verbs left to right. First value goes to the first verb, second to the second, and so on.

Run this. Then add one more line that prints: `"Go version: 1.21, released: 2023"`
expectedOutput: Alex is 25 years old with a score of 98.5

codeChecks.message: Add a Printf line that prints the Go version and release year.
Replace the TODO line with actual code.

successMessage: One format string, many values. The order of values matches the order of verbs — always.

title: Build a formatted profile card
instruction:
No starter code.

Using only what you learned in this chapter, build a profile card that outputs exactly this:

```
===== PROFILE =====
Name:     Alex
Age:      25
Score:    98.50
Active:   true
===================
```

Use `Printf` with width formatting for the label column. Use `%.2f` for the score.
hint: fmt.Printf("%-10s%s\n", "Name:", name)  — %-10s left-aligns the label in 10 characters. Use %.2f for score.

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Use %.2f to format the score to 2 decimal places.
Add the header and footer lines with ===== PROFILE ===== and ===================

successMessage: Clean, aligned, professional output. This is the difference between code that works and code that looks like it was made by someone who knows what they are doing.
